key_facts: Matter 5 (STK-CV-UF-2026-0001032, San Joaquin Superior Court, Hon. George J. Abdallah) asserts state tort claims — fraud/misrepresentation, negligence, false imprisonment, intentional infliction of emotional distress, and economic interference — against private defendants HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. and its clinician Katie Palacios, arising from a financially conflicted, clinically retaliatory course of conduct within Plaintiff's mandatory [REDACTED] treatment program. HOPE operates a fee-for-service billing structure charging $85 per group session and $600 per polygraph examination, generating an estimated financial incentive of approximately $9,690 per year to retain each parolee rather than discharge them — a blanket retention policy that was admitted under oath in the Dwyer proceeding and expressly condemned by that court, establishing prior judicial condemnation of the precise practice Defendants continued to apply to Plaintiff. Palacios's specific clinical conduct forms the factual core of the case: at the mid-2025 Clinical Treatment Meeting (CTM), she labeled Plaintiff a 'ringleader' whose speech encouraged others to speak and used that characterization as adverse clinical input; during group sessions she facilitated after the previously assigned clinician abruptly resigned, she remarked that Plaintiff was 'doing better when he spoke less' — a statement whose chilling effect was independently corroborated by clinician Ms. Jackie; and in early April 2025 she jointly revoked Plaintiff's standard 10-minute grace period alongside parole agent Derrick, declaring 'You are clearly abusing the 10-minute as an excuse to show up late.' Palacios's retaliatory characterizations were formally incorporated into the November 2025 Discharge Review (Form 1502-DR), creating a documented chain of retaliation from clinical assessment through official parole records, and her dual role as both the assessing clinician and a direct financial beneficiary of Plaintiff's continued retention establishes an irreconcilable conflict of interest supporting both the fraud and negligence theories. Because HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. and Palacios are private defendants — not government actors — no government tort claim under the California Government Claims Act was required prior to filing, and neither qualified immunity nor sovereign immunity is available to shield Defendants from liability, conferring a decisive strategic advantage over every parallel matter in the litigation cluster that names government actors.
procedural_status: Matter 5 complaint was filed on February 10, 2026 in San Joaquin Superior Court (STK-CV-UF-2026-0001032). The court approved Plaintiff's fee waiver and issued summons. Katie Palacios was personally served on February 24, 2026 by the San Joaquin County Sheriff; the POS-010 proof of service was filed with the court on March 3, 2026, establishing Palacios's original response deadline of March 26, 2026. On March 26, 2026, HOPE Psychotherapy Inc., d/b/a HOPE Program, was separately served via a signed Notice of Acknowledgment and Receipt (NAR) returned executed, rendering service complete as to all named defendants in Matter 5. The original Palacios state response deadline of March 26, 2026 passed without appearance; on March 27, 2026, the firm Sims, Lawrence & Broghammer (attorneys Cynthia G. Lawrence, SBN 148927, and Bryan L. Malone, SBN 144082, Roseville, CA) contacted Plaintiff to request a 30-day extension. The parties stipulated to extend Palacios's state response deadline to April 27, 2026 — this extension is expressly tethered to the state case (STK-CV-UF-2026-1032) only and does NOT extend to or affect the parallel federal matter 2:26-cv-00004-DJC-JDP. Separately, in the federal case (Matter 3), the same defense firm filed a Stipulation and Proposed Order (ECF Doc. 22, filed April 2, 2026) extending Palacios's federal response deadline to April 6, 2026, with all defenses expressly reserved — that extension is a wholly distinct instrument governing only the federal proceeding. Palacios's response in this state matter remains due April 27, 2026; given the express reservation of all defenses in the SLB stipulation, a Rule 12 demurrer or motion to strike is anticipated rather than a plain answer. The Case Management Conference (CMC) before Hon. George J. Abdallah is set for October 13, 2026.
evidence: Dwyer order — judicial condemnation of HOPE's blanket retention policy, admitted under oath; establishes prior finding of unlawful practice directly applicable to Defendants' continued conduct

Alameda Contract — billing rate documentation establishing HOPE's fee-for-service structure: $85 per group session, $600 per polygraph examination, ~$9,690/year financial incentive per retained parolee
HOPE billing records — corroborate the fee-for-service financial conflict of interest and document revenues generated from Plaintiff's continued retention

Form 1502-DR (November 2025 Discharge Review Report) — incorporates Palacios's 'ringleader' and Derrick's 'disruptive' characterizations; facially defective; used to deny discharge and justify escalated supervision; signed and approved by Noguchi

CTM records (mid-2025 Clinical Treatment Meeting) — document Palacios's 'ringleader' label applied to Plaintiff, Derrick's negative oral report on Plaintiff's in-session speech, and Noguchi's on-the-spot denial of reassignment

HOPE Group Session Camera Recordings — sessions at Stockton facility (5403 N. Pershing Ave., Suite 200) were conducted in camera-equipped rooms; recordings of sessions Palacios facilitated after clinician resignation are direct evidence of her speech-suppression statements and conduct
Palacios 'ringleader' characterization records — clinical documentation, CTM minutes, and any notes reflecting the label applied to Plaintiff and its downstream incorporation into parole records

'Doing better when spoke less' observation by Palacios — on-record statement made during facilitated group session suppressing Plaintiff's in-session speech; subject to camera recording and witness recollection

Clinician Ms. Jackie independent corroboration — contemporaneous observation by independent clinician noting behavioral change in Plaintiff consistent with chilling effect of Palacios's speech-suppression remark

FedEx records — job offer, training schedule (Modesto, April 1, 2025, $20/hr, ~30 hrs/week), scheduling accommodation denial, and forced resignation (~April 17, 2025) — economic harm proximately linked to Palacios and Derrick's joint grace-period revocation
CDCR Form 22 (abandoned agent-change request) — corroborates intimidation environment Palacios helped create and documents chilling effect on Plaintiff's exercise of administrative remedies
POS-010 (filed March 3, 2026) — proof of personal service on Katie Palacios in Matter 5 by San Joaquin County Sheriff on February 24, 2026
NAR (Notice of Acknowledgment and Receipt) — executed by HOPE Psychotherapy Inc., d/b/a HOPE Program, confirming service on March 26, 2026; establishes service complete as to all named defendants

SLB Stipulation (state case, April 27, 2026 deadline) — written record tethered exclusively to STK-CV-UF-2026-1032; confirms Palacios's extended state response deadline and expressly preserves all defenses, signaling anticipated dispositive motion

SLB Stipulation (federal Doc. 22, filed April 2, 2026, April 6, 2026 deadline) — separate and distinct stipulation in federal case 2:26-cv-00004-DJC-JDP; all defenses reserved; confirms parallel defense posture and cross-matter monitoring obligation
risk: Matter 5 presents a markedly favorable risk profile compared to every parallel matter naming government defendants. Neither HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. nor Katie Palacios can invoke qualified immunity or sovereign immunity — the foundational defenses available to CDCR agents and administrators in Matters 3 and 4 — because both are private actors contracting with the state, not government employees. This immunity gap is the single most significant structural advantage in Cluster 2 litigation: Plaintiff may proceed directly to the merits on all five tort theories without navigating the threshold immunity barriers that dominate the federal § 1983 track. The Dwyer judicial condemnation of HOPE's blanket retention policy functions as a near-dispositive estoppel on the core retention theory: because HOPE admitted the policy under oath and a court has already condemned it, Defendants face an uphill defense on the element of wrongful conduct, and any continued enforcement of the same policy after Dwyer strengthens Plaintiff's showing of willfulness and bad faith. The fee-for-service financial conflict of interest — $9,690/year per retained parolee — is not merely background context; it is affirmative evidence of fraud and negligence, directly attacking the reliability of Palacios's clinical judgment and HOPE's institutional discharge assessments, and it supports a punitive damages finding by establishing a pecuniary motive for the retaliatory conduct. HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. is additionally exposed to potential state-actor liability under West v. Atkins (487 U.S. 42 (1988)) in the parallel federal Matter 6: because HOPE performs a delegated governmental function — court- and parole-mandated [REDACTED] treatment — it may be deemed a state actor subject to § 1983 liability, and any adverse ruling on that theory in the federal forum would carry significant collateral implications for HOPE's defense posture in this state case. The prospect of punitive damages is real and well-founded: Palacios's documented retaliation (the 'ringleader' label, the speech-suppression remark, the coordinated grace-period revocation), combined with HOPE's continued operation of a blanket retention policy already condemned by a court and a billing structure that financially rewards retention over discharge, satisfies the 'malice, oppression, or fraud' standard under California Civil Code § 3294. Finally, Matter 5 benefits from powerful cross-matter evidentiary reinforcement: the Dwyer order, Alameda Contract, Form 1502-DR, CTM records, and HOPE session recordings are common evidence shared with Matter 3 (federal, § 1983, First Amendment retaliation) and Matter 6 (federal, § 1983, Procedural Due Process); favorable findings on HOPE's retention practices or Palacios's clinical conduct in any one matter create collateral estoppel and reputational pressure that amplifies Plaintiff's leverage across all proceedings simultaneously.
